- NMISTORY OP GALWAY, alles q . 4. The Mercantile Coffe Room, Was. opened about the year 1792, by subscription. The original list of members was chiefly composed of the gentry, merchants, and respectable shopkeepers of the town. The establishment has recently branched out into two separate reading-rooms, which receive the principal London and Dublin 4 papers, and also the éhree newspapers published in the town, viz.: the Con; naught Journal, Galway Chronicle, and Weekly Advertiser.* These latter : publications, though they cannot be adduced as a proof of increasing wealth or commerce, afford, however, unquestionable evidence of the anxiety of the f ' town’s-people for information and improvement. It has been observed that, ever since the mitigation of the penal Jaws, in 1778, ; the benefits of education have gradually extended over this part of Ireland. ; Many of the inhabitants of Galway and several gentlemen of the surrounding 4 districts are distinguished for polite and elegant information. Book-shops and ' circulating libraries have increased, and a love for reading and literary taste is : happily becoming more general. This is particularly observable amongst those ' in the middle walk of life, a numerous and respectable class, which includes a a great part of the population of the town.” \ 5. Character, Manners, Customs, &c. q Having thus far attempted a delineation of the principal features of Galway, » - its topography, constitution, commerce, public buildings, institutions, &c. the 4 whole shall be concluded with a bricf view of the inhabitants, their general (| character, manners, customs, &c. This is a subject which, for many reasons, f the author would willingly decline; but as it is one deemed indispensible in Ik works of’ this nature, he must, though concisely, and with diffidence, comply with ‘ the general custom. ## WISTORY OF GALWAY: 

shall be adhered to, the natives of Galway need be under no great apprehensions from the most minute investigation. ‘The former inhabitants, it is true, have been charged with ‘ possessing an inordinate quantity of pride,” or, as described by lord Clanricarde in 1641, they “were not without a large portion of pride, and particularly piqued themselves on entertaining high notions of honor.” This feeling, if not originally acquired, certainly suffered no diminution during their long continued intercourse with the Spanish nation :* some will even assert that it has been communicated to their descendants, and that it may be very visibly perecived amongst many of them at the present day.? From this source most prebably sprung the practice of duelling,* for which the inhabitants of the town and county of Galway have been heretofore so remarkable, and which, even still, is far from being eradicated. This custom is rather singular for being so general among a people well known to be religious by habit; but like the celebrated chevalier Bayard, «the knight without fear or reproach,” it’ may he said of many an individual here, “that he always heard mass before he fought a duel.” As civilization, however, advances, it is observable that this practice is eradualiy declining. Another propensity, which has been also pointed out as tolerably general in this part of Ireland, is, an inclination for law, or, according to some, an immodcrate love of litigation.’ This, however, admits of some qualitication. It may, with as much propriety, be termed a desire to obtain strict and impartial justice; and we have the authority of Sir John Davis for asserting, « that no nation under the sun did love equal and indifferent justice better than the trish.” But it must still be conceded, that the decline of both, or either of these propensities, and the increase of industrious habits, would have the most salutary effects on the morals, happiness, and prosperity. of . the’ people. -’ A gentleman well acquainted with Galway, and who had, for many years, resided in Spain, frequently mentioned to the author, that he thought he perceived, in many instances, a striking coincidence between the manners of the people, particularly those of the middle rank, in bowi places. Tt is rather remarkable that somewhat a similar observation was made, many years ago, of the inhabitants of Limerick, to the author of the Philosophical Survey of * A Spaniard of ancient descent, and particularly a duble as those which are founded in virtue and honor, Costin, thinks himself the most important being in ©The county of Galway was formerly famous for such gatires bat, accordimg to one of our popular writers, fiziting gentlemen as blue-Blaze-Devil-Bob, Ninetecn“yiexactedheutous as this pride is, it is productive of the most Duel-Dick, Hair-Trirger-Pat, and Peather-Spring-Ned; humane qusdiics:and virtuonsit inspiressentiments;the nationit beingwithseldomgenerous,found batwith thesetriumphhonorableby ancicutagnowensfamilies;wouldtheybe noare lovgersinkingcitedfast that a Spitish nobleman, gentleman, or even trader, is into oblivion.— Ldgeworth on Professional Education, guilty of a mean action.” fit has been observed tiat no city in Ireland, even of ¢ A late come writer of some celebrity, describing the much greater extent, is better supplied with attorneys selimpo:tance of one of his charactirs, tells us that he than Gzlway. i ; ’ ; HISTORY OF GALWAY. 315 i the South of Ireland.—(Vide p. 221.)—Among the lower orders here, as in the i last mentioned city, many of what are termed the old Milesian habits are still j prevalent. It must, however, be confessed that this class requires much improve!bh J ment. Indolence is. perhaps the cause ofaw all their. defects;a but this. arises. not so | much from inclination as necessity, there not being sufficient sources of profitable 14 employment for the industrious poor, Small inns and_ tippling-houses are » 4 numerous, and even on the Sabbath are very indecorously kept open during the ” hours of divine worship. The evening also of this sacred day is not unfrequently : profaned by drunkenness and riot. Several old and vulgar ceremonies, formerly in esteem, have long since disappeared, It is many years since the savage | ¥° practice of bull-baiting has been laid aside: chanticleer need no longer tremble | at the approach of Shrovetide, and the public cock-pit, formerly in such request, now lies deservedly neglected.—To conclude, the population of Galway, with :5 many faults, will be also found’ to possess several excellent qualities, and, if 14 adequately encouraged, would prove in no degree inferior to that of any other i part of the British dominions. | 6. Public Amusements, Sc. ) J The theatre is situate in Kirwan’s-lane,.which is one of the most confined and } inconvenient parts of the town. The house is small, but the scenery is esteemed : elegant; and, from the taste and attention of Mr.Macartney, the accommodations ; are as complete as the nature and size of the building will permit. The most i 3 celebrated performers, viz. Barry, Mossop, Kemble, Cooke, Siddons, Walstein, ; and O’Neill, have occasionally visited these boards. The facetious Smithson had | long been a favorite; and the exertions of Mr. Clarke to contribute to the ra:: tionalhowever,entertainmentin an open ofand thecentralpublicsituation,are deservingappearsof necessary.every success.‘The Aassembly-roomnew theatre, _ i § in Middle-street, frequently displays an assemblage of native beauty, elegance : and fashion, which would grace the drawing-rooms of a court.". occasional concerts and incidental public exhibitions, are the only species of amusements which engage the attention of the inhabitants of Galway, If the green, or Meyrick-square, (which has lately become a favorite promenade with the Iadies,) be excepted, there is no public walk worthy of the town; but there are some agrecable rides towards the west, whence the picturesque scenery of the bay, and the county of Clare mountains appear to considerable advantage. 

7: Country Seats. : There are several fine seats in the vicinity of Galway. Of these the principal are :—Ardfry, the beautiful seat of the right honorablé lord Wallscourt, situate near the new harbour, to which vessels resort from the road of Galway in tempestuous weather, and ride in perfect safety; Tyrone House, the seat of Arthur I'french St. George, esq. which commands a charming prospect of the bay of Galway and the islands of Arran; Renvile, the seat of Philip Lynch Athy, esq.; Oranmore Castle, the residence of Walter Blake, esq.; Frenchfort, the seat of Michael Blake, esq.; Merlin Park, the fine seat of Charles Blake, esq. adjacent to the ruined castle of Doughisky; Well Park, the seat of Mr. Macnamara; Merview, the scat of Mr. Joyce; Menlo Castle, the ancient residence of the Blake family; Dangan, the former scat of the Martin family, (this was universally acknowledged to be one of the most delightful residences in the kingdom, but it has of lute years been suffered to go considerably to decay ;) Newcastle, the property of Robert Persse, esq. conveniently situated near the river, opposite the ruined castle of Terrilan, the once noble residence of the earls of Clanricarde; Rahoon, the seat of James Bodkin, esy.; West Lodge, the seat of our late venerable and respected recorder, James O'Hara, esq.3 Barna, the highly improved and elegant seat of Marcus Blake Lynch, esq. which for situation and beauty of prospect stands unrivalled. Galway gives the title of viscount to the noble family of Moncton.

nullus cujuscunque status, gradus seu conditionis consuctudines, ibidem ab antiquo consuetas et fuerint, locum-tenente ct cancellario suis terre approbatas, manutenendas et ministrandasin oinninostre predicte omnino exceptis, villam nostram bus, prout decet: Necnon pro majore securitati et predictam, nisi per licentiam, assensum et supersalva gardia dicte ville de Galway, voluit et ordivisionem superioris, prepositi, ballivorum, burgennavit, quod nullus, cujuscunque status, gradus scu sium et communitatis ejusdem ville, ingrediatur conditionis fuerit, eandem villam de Galway, nisi quoquo modo, prout in cisdem literis de confirmaper licenciam, assensum et supervisionem eorum tlone dicti avi nostri similiter plenius continetur. — majoris, ballivorum et communitatis ejusdem ville, Cumygue ctiamn postea, Ricardus, nuper Rex ingrediatur quoquo modo in futuro. Preterea, de Anvlie tertius, per literas suas patentes, datas apud uberiori gratia sua, ac pro majore securitate et Westmonasterium quinto decimo die Decembris, salva gardia dicte ville de Galway, voluit et ordianno regni sui secundy, premissa considerantes, navit ac pro se et heredibus suis, quantum in ipso et ut predicta villa de Galway, inimicos ac rebelles fuit, concessit quod extunc, nec Dominus ME IWilpredictos forcius resisteret, ce gratia sua speciali, liam, Dominus de Clanrickarde, nec heredes sui, onfines et singulas literas patentes superius recitatas, haberent seu haberet aliquod gubernium, seu et omnia et singula in eisdem contenta, rata habenaliquam potestatem infra dictam villam de Galway, tes et grata, eas, prose et heredibus suis, quantum ad ibidem aliquod agendun, percipiendum, exigenin ipso fuit, acceptavit, approbavit, ratificavit et dum, ordinandum seu disponendum, per terram seu tune superiori, preposito, ballivis, burgensibus et ° per aquam, in quantum ipse dominus, et predecescommunitati dicte ville de Galway, confirmavit; sores sui, percipere et exigere consueverunt ab ac etiam omnimodas actiones et demanda, ac antiquo; absque licencia speciali, ac per assensum omne id quod ad ipsum Regem Ricardum, versus et supervisionem majoris, ballivorum et communieosdem superiorem, prepositum, ballivos, burgentatis dicte ville de Galway; quibus dedit, concessit ses, Communitatem vel successores suos, ratione et attribuit plenariam potestatem et auctoritatem, premissorum, vel alicujus eorundem, pertinuit, eandem villam regendam et gubernandam, bene et seu pertinere poterit, eis pardonavit, remisit Jaudabiliter in omnibus, prout decet, prout in eiset relaxavit: Et ulterius, pro majore securitate dem literis suis patentibus plenius continetur. eorundem tune superioris, prepositi, ballivorum, Jamque nos (Hen. octavits.) premissa conside+ burgensium et communitatis ville predicte, et sucrantes, et ad auxiliam, relevamen et confortatiocessorum suorum predictorum, de uberiori gratia nem ville nostre de Galway predicte, ut mercatores sua, concessit, quod ipsi et successores sui, omnes et alli, in eadem villa ad commorandum et inhabiet singulas dictas consuetudines et custumas, in tandum magis animarentur et confortarentur, ad dictis literis patentibus de decimo octavo die maliciam inimicorum et rebellium quorumcunque, Novembris, prefatis preposito, ballivis et commuet precipue parcium illarum, forcius resistendum, nitati, ut predictum est, factas, specificatas, de de gratia nostra speciali, omnes et singulas literas rebus venalibus ad eandem villam de Galway per patentes, superius recitatas, ac omnia et singula in terram sive per aquam venientibus, seu ab eadem eisdem contenta, rata hatentes et grata, ca pro transeuntibus, per se, vel per ministros suos ad nobis, heredibus et successoribus nostris, quantum hoc deputandos, in hac parte, de tempore intemin nobis est, acceptamus, approbamus et ratificapus, capere, recipere, levare habere ct remittere mus, et nune majori, ballivis, burgensibus et compossint, libere, quiecte, bene et pacifice imperpemunitati dicte ville nostre de Galway, heredibus tuum, absque impetitione seu contradictione ipsius et successoribus suis, tenore presentium, damus, Regis, heredum seu ministrorum suorum quorumconcedimus et confirmamus: ac etiam, omnimodas cunquecirca muragium; ita semper quodet pavagium denariiejusdem inde prevenientes,ville, et non actionesversus eosdemet demanda, majorem, acballivos,omneburgensesid quod etadcom-nos alibi, ut predictum est, expendantur: Et ulterius, munitatem, vel corum aliquem vel aliquos, heredes pro majore securitate et salva gardia ville de Galvel successores suos, ratione premissorum, vel way predicte, idem nuper Rex voluit et ordinavit, alicujus eorundem, pertinebat seu pertinere poterit, ac per easdem literas suas patentes, licenciam eis, et corum cuilibet, pardonavimus, remisimus dedit et concessit superiori, preposito, ballivis et et relaxavimus, ac per presentes pardonamus, communitati ejusdem ville nostre, quod ipsi, annuremittimus et relaxamus: Et ulterius, pro majori atim ianperpetuum, eligere ex eorum communi securitate eorundem nunc majoris, ballivorum, assensu, wun majorem et duos ballivos, infraeandem burgensium et communitatis ville nostre de Galway villam de Galway, prout fieri solet in villa Bristoll, predicte, heredum et successoruin suorum predicad eandem villam de Galway bene et laudabiliter torum, de uberiori gratia nostra, ac ex certa scientia regendam et gubernandam, ac leges suas ct bonas et mero motu nostris, damus et concedimus eisdem B

Cum per suggestionem et informationem majoris, Hibernici inimici nostri, nacionem de O'Fflartyes, ballivorum, burgensium et communitatis ville et alii penes et juxta dictam villam commorantes, nostre Galvie, in regno nostro Hibernie, factas, in ecclesia predicta eorum mortuos sepelire conapparet quod Episcopus Romanus auctoritatem sueverunt, et alongé a diutine usi sunt, pretextu nostram supremi capitis ecclesie Anglicane et et colore cujus, villam nostram predictam intrantes, Tlibernicane falso ct injuste usurpans, et exercens; subditos nostros ejusdem in discrimen non modiquedam ecclesiastica beneficia et emolumenta, cum vite sue, et amissionis ac deperdicionis ville viz. vicariam de Galluey, vicariam de Marey, vicanostre predicte, sepe inducunt; et quia sunt et rias et rectorias de Rawin et Mocullen, vicariam existunt in suburbiis, et extra muros ejusdem ville, et rectoriam de Fororanmore, vicariam et rectotres ecclesie et tria cimiteria trium nuper domuum riam de Roscam, vicarias de Clare et Killcomen prioratuum, sive monasteriorum fratrum mendivicariam de Srowre, in diocesi Anaghtuamensi, ac cantium, quorum unum vocatur monasterium P vicariam de Skryne in diocesi Tuamensi; necnon Sancti Francisci, secundum, Sancti Dominici, terquandam parcellam decimarum in villa de Galvey, tium, Sancti Augustini, ad usum sepulture valde , predicte, vulgariter dictam, ‘“ the bushop’s quarnecessaria, nos easdem ecclesias et cimiteria, ad ter,” et omnes et singulos fructus, provenciones, usum sepulture, ut premittitur, ecclesie, predicte redditus, procuraciones, decimas, oblationes, altedare, concedere, unire, annectere, appropriare, ragia, penciones, porciones, ct omnia alia proficua, incorporare, et realtier ac perpetuo consolidare, commoditates, et emolumenta quecumque, vicariadignaremur: Et ulterius, per eosdem majorem, rum et rectoriarum predictarum, cumomnibus suis ballivos, burgenses, et communitatem, nobis simipertinenciis, ecclesie Sancti Nicholai ville predicte, liter est supplicatum, quatenus dictam ecclesiam ad cultum divinum in eadem augmentandum, dedit, Sancti Nicholai in collegium sive ecclesiam colleconcessit, univit, annexit, appropriavit, et incorgiatam auctoritate nostra predicta transponere, et poravit, et consolidavit realiter et perpetuo, mutare dignaremur. Nos, supplicacionibus et : Quibusquidem fructibus, provencionibus, reddipetitionibus humilibus dictorum majoris, ballivo: tibus, procurationibus, decimis, oblationibus, alterum, burgensium, et communitatis, in consideraragiis, pencionibus, porcionibus, proficuis, comtione allegiancie et debite obediencie sue, annueri, moditatibus, et emolumentis rectoriarum et vicaet consentiri volentes; ac diurnum cultum, et riarum predictarum, novem presbiteri, sacerdotes, alia omnia ad eundem pertinencia, modo quo sive ministri, ad sacramenta et sacramentalia in licite possit augmentari; necnon securitatem et ecclesia predicta ministranda et alia ad divinum salvam custodiam dictorum majoris, ballivorum, i cultum pertinencia, exequenda et facienda, burgensium et communitatis, intimo animo, prout fi sustinabuntur, et ad presens sustentantur, ac aucRegem decct, maxime cupientes Ht toritatem et jurisdictionem Romani pontificis sic, bene et fideliter erga nos gerendum,; moite, Johannem O’Brangan et Edmundum in eodem collegio sive ecclesia collegiata minisO’Flartie jam similiter ibidem servientes, et ministrantium; ac easdem ordinaciones et statuta abrotrantes, vicarios chorales in collegio sive ecclesia gare, alterare et mutare, et alia in eorum loco collegiata predicta, ordinamus et constituimus, componere, et addere, totiens quotiens, sibi videvolentes quod idem Patricius Blake, et successores bitur expedire:—Ac etiam concessimus et licen+ sui, ut gardiani, et predicti Patricius Kerewane ct tiam dedimus, ac per presentes concedimus et ‘Thomas Frenche, Derby O'Hoysshyne Johannes licentiam damus, prefatis majori, ballivis, burgenTalman, Derby O’Nowane, Johannes Dermoite, sibus et communitati, quod ipsi, et eorum sucJohannes O'Brangan et Edmundus O’Flartie et cessores, imperpetuum, quolibet anno, ad festum successores sui, ut vicarii chorales collegii, sive Sancti Petri predicti, eligere poterint, et eligant - ecclesie collegiate predicte, sint de cetero accepunam idoneam personam in gardianum collegii, tati et reputati:—Et volumus preterea et concesive ecclesie collegiate predicte, habendum sibi dimus, quod collegium sive ecclesiam collegiatam pro uno anno integro; ac eundem gardianun, predictam, extune sit de uno ‘gardiano et octo necnon vicarios pro tempore existentes, et corunt , vicariis choralibus unum corpus, incorporatum re quemlibet, si ita eis videbitur, amovendos, depriet nomine, habeantque successionem perpetuam; vandos et deponendos, et alium sive alios, in ac predictum Patricium Blake gardianum, preejus et eorum loco, constituendos, nominandos dictos Patricium Kerewane, Thomam Frenche, ct assignandos de tempore in tempus, ac etiam DerbyO'Nowane,O'Hoysshyne,JohannemJohannemDermoite,Talman,JohannemDerby prouteosdemcausa gardianumet occasio et vicarios, etfuerit ct corumservicrit, quemlibet,castiO'Brangan et Edmundum O’Flartie, vicarios chogandos, corrigendos, et modo debito punicnrales collegii nostri, sive ecclesie collegiate de dos; et quod ipsi major, ballivi, burgenses comGalway, nominamus, facimus, constituimus et munitas et successores sui, imperpetuum, cum ordinamus, incorporamus, decernimus, et declagardianus predictus sive aliquis vicariorum preramus, imperpetuum, per presentes:—Et quod dictorum, pro tempore existentium, obierit, pergardianus et vicarii chorales, collegii nostri sive mutaverit, resignaverit vel alio aliquo quocunque ecclesie collegiate predicte, ac successores sui, modo, ab servicio in collegio sive ecclesia collegardianus et vicarii chorales, collegii regii de giata predicta, recesserit, alium quemcunque, loco Galway, perpetuo nuncupentur, nominentur, voipsius decedentis, vel alio modo ab codem rececentur et acceptantur; et quod idem gardianus, dentis, eligere, et eundem, sic electum, in realem et vicarii chorales, ac successores sui sint persone possessionem ejusdem ponere possint, absque habiles et in lege capaces per idem nomen placialiqua prosecutione, penes nos, heredes et suctari et implacitari, respondere et responderi, ac cessores nostros, vel penes quemcumque alium, prosequi, defendere ct defendi in onmibus et sinpro licentia et concensu in ea parte concedendis gulis placitis, sectis, querelis et demandis, motis vel obtinendis. Sciatis etiam quod nos de gratia et movendis, in quibuscunque curiis nostris, herenostra speciali, ac ex certa scientia et mero motu -dum ect successorum nostrorum, et aliorum quonostris, auctoritate nostra regia, et supremi capitis rumcunque; ac coram quibuscunque judicibus, ecclesie Anglicane et Hibernicane, ex plenitujusticiatiis, et aliis officiariis nostris, heredum et dine potestatis, qua fungimur in hac parte, dictas successorum nostrorum, et aliorum: quorumcunrectorias ac vigarias, et eorum quantibet, necnon que; ## XXxvi 

## Number VII. 

The parliament of England having ordained, that in consequence of the losses sustained by the inhabitants of Gloucester during the siege of that city by the royal forces, such part of the forfeited lands, &c. in Ireland as should be valued at 10,0007. should be conveyed unto Anthony Edwards and Thomas Whitcombe, in trust for said inhabitants :—afterwards, in an additional act passed in the parliament, begun and holden at Westminster, the 17th September, 1656, ordered that the lord deputy and council of Ireland should set out to said trustees, out of the forfeited lands in the province of Connaught and county of Clare, and the forfeited houses in the citty of Galloway and liberties thereof, so much, as at the rate of six years purchase, should amount to that sum. 

A PARTICULAR OR SuRVEY OF THE LANDS SET OUT IN PURSUANCE OF SAID ADDITIONAL Act, IN THE CouNTY OF THE TOWN OF GALWAY.
